data.title: KuCoin Operator Hit With Permanent US Ban After $500,000 CFTC Settlement Over Unregistered Trading Access
data.description:
KuCoin Faces Permanent Ban As Us Enforcement Settles

The "People’s Exchange" is officially closing its doors to the United States after its operator, Peken Global Limited, was ordered to pay a $500,000 civil penalty.

This settlement with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) turns a temporary exit into an indefinite ban.

A consent order approved by the US District Court for the Southern District of New York marks the end of a long-running legal battle, ensuring the exchange cannot serve US residents unless it undergoes a rigorous and unlikely registration as a foreign board of trade.

Crypto exchange KuCoin will pay $500,000 to settle U.S. regulatory allegations that it operated an unregistered offshore platform allowing U.S. residents to trade. #Crypto#Bitcoin#US

— ZettaWire (@ZettaWire) March 30, 2026

Why Did The Cftc Settle For A Smaller Fine

While the $500,000 civil penalty might look small for an exchange with a $1.7 billion daily trading volume, the figure is deceptive.

The court opted not to seek disgorgement because Peken Global had already been hit hard in a parallel criminal case.

In January 2025, the firm pleaded guilty to operating an unlicensed money transmitting business, a move that came with nearly $297 million in penalties and forfeitures.

🚨BREAKING: #KuCoin Settles with 🇺🇸DOJ Over Unlicensed Money Transmitter Charges

⦿ Agreed to pay $297 million in penalties.
⦿ Will exit the U.S. market for 2 years.
⦿ Founders Chun Gan ("Michael") and Ke Tang ("Eric") removed from management.
⦿ Founders indicted, but…

— Crypto India (@CryptooIndia) January 28, 2025

The agency explained that it refrained from demanding more money because the company cooperated with the investigation and was already facing massive financial losses from the Department of Justice case.

By stacking these actions, authorities managed to drain the exchange’s US profits through criminal law while using civil orders to lock the gate behind them.

Federal Court Enters Permanent Injunction Against Peken Global Limited: https://t.co/ceUdshuxK5

— CFTC (@CFTC) March 30, 2026

Did Sham Compliance Procedures Lead To This Exit

The core of the government’s frustration lay in how KuCoin handled its 1.5 million US users.

Despite claiming to be a global platform, investigators found that the exchange only introduced know-your-customer (KYC) requirements in August 2023.

Even then, these rules were not applied to existing accounts, creating a massive loophole for US traders.

The CFTC argued these were "sham" procedures designed to circumvent regulations while the exchange pocketed approximately $110 million in trading fees from Americans.

The Department of Justice estimated the total fees generated from US participants was even higher, reaching at least $184.5 million.

DOJ says KuCoin served 1.5 million U.S. users and earned ~$184.5 M in fees.
All while avoiding compliance duties that regulated exchanges must follow.
This is why the penalty was so steep — nearly $297 M total.

— Shaun Horne (@shaunchorne) October 23, 2025

What Happens To The Other Kucoin Entities

As part of the resolution, the court dismissed remaining claims against three other affiliated companies: Mek Global Limited, PhoenixFin PTE Ltd., and Flashdot Limited.

By focusing the final injunction on Peken Global, the regulator simplified the enforcement process.

This marks a rare sequential victory for US regulators, who successfully transitioned from a heavy-hitting criminal prosecution to a permanent market ban.

For KuCoin, which operates out of the Seychelles, the Cayman Islands, and Singapore, the American market is now a chapter of the past.

The ruling replaces a previous two-year minimum withdrawal period with a permanent injunction, effectively ending its status as a player in the US crypto landscape.

data.title: Maryland Hacker Faces 30 Years After $54M Uranium Finance Heist, Spends Loot on Pokemon Cards
data.description:
Double hacks devastate DeFi exchange

A Maryland man allegedly drained $54 million from DeFi platform Uranium Finance through audacious smart contract hacks — then splurged the stolen crypto on rare trading cards and a piece of the Wright brothers’ airplane that went to the moon. Now, Jonathan Spalletta could spend decades behind bars.

who is Jonathan Spalletta, aka "Cthulhon" and "Jspalletta," the 36-year-old Maryland man behind the Uranium Finance hacks?

> accused of carrying out a two-stage exploit against Uranium Finance in April 2021

> first, he kept depositing and withdrawing in a mispriced pool to…

— Sophia Hodlberg (@sophiaHodlberg) March 30, 2026

Jonathan Spalletta, 36, is charged in New York’s Southern District with computer fraud, carrying a maximum of 10 years, and money laundering, carrying up to 20 years, after allegedly executing two devastating attacks on Uranium Finance in 2021.

U.S. Attorney Jay Clayton condemned the scheme as “a brazen destruction of a cryptocurrency exchange”. He also highlighted Spalletta’s alleged dismissal of his victims where he claimed that “Crypto is just fake internet money anyway.”

The first hack in April 2021 exploited a reward calculation flaw, allowing Spalletta to siphon $1.4 million from the liquidity pool.

“Stealing from a crypto exchange is stealing – the claim that ‘crypto is different’ does not chang that,’” said U.S. Attorney Jay Clayton. “For the victims, there is nothing different about having your money taken.”https://t.co/jSaPJ0F5LRpic.twitter.com/TbQ1mLfOYp

— US Attorney SDNY (@SDNYnews) March 30, 2026

Weeks later, he discovered a second vulnerability and executed a staggering $53.3 million heist, forcing Uranium Finance to shut down due to depleted funds.

(1/2)‼️ Uranium migration has been exploited, the following address has 50m in it The only thing that matters is keeping the funds on BSC, everyone please start tweeting this address to Binance immediately asking them to stop transfers.

— Uranium Finance (@UraniumFinance) April 28, 2021

Authorities recovered $31 million tied to the first hack in February 2025, demonstrating that even years later, blockchain forensics can trace stolen crypto.

Stolen crypto fuels collector frenzy

Spalletta allegedly funneled his stolen crypto into a remarkable collector’s spree. Reports detail purchases of rare Pokémon and Magic: The Gathering trading cards worth millions, alongside a historic piece of Wright brothers airplane fabric that Neil Armstrong carried to the moon during the 1969 Apollo 11 mission.

who is Jonathan Spalletta, aka "Cthulhon" and "Jspalletta," the 36-year-old Maryland man behind the Uranium Finance hacks?

> accused of carrying out a two-stage exploit against Uranium Finance in April 2021

> first, he kept depositing and withdrawing in a mispriced pool to…

— Sophia Hodlberg (@sophiaHodlberg) March 30, 2026

Additional luxury acquisitions reportedly accompanied these purchases. The acquisition of such a historic artifact highlights the surreal extremes of crypto crime — turning digital heists into tangible, high-profile collector bragging rights.

The case underscores the U.S. government’s zero-tolerance approach to smart contract manipulation, treating blockchain theft with the same severity as traditional cybercrime or bank heists.

With potential exposure of up to 30 years in prison, Spalletta’s audacious exploits may be legendary, but the courtroom could bring an abrupt end to his spree.

As decentralized finance continues to grow in 2026, Uranium Finance’s collapse serves as a stark warning: vulnerabilities in smart contracts can translate into multi-million-dollar federal investigations.

JUST IN: 🇺🇸 CFTC Chairman Michael Selig says "the CFTC stands ready to implement purpose-fit digital asset regulations to help ensure the US remains the crypto capital of the world." #Cardano$ADA$NIGHTpic.twitter.com/7agRQBI5JI

— Angry Crypto Show (@angrycryptoshow) March 7, 2026

Spalletta’s moon-bound Wright brothers fabric may capture headlines, but the harsh legal consequences he faces remind the crypto world that audacious theft carries real-world repercussions.

data.title: Retail investors' first "rebellion" against Nvidia: The AI ​​bull market may really be about to change.
data.description:
Author: US Stock Investment Network; Source: X, @TradesMax

x

Over the past year, almost everyone has been doing the same thing—buying NVIDIA.

Since ChatGPT ignited the AI ​​wave, this company has soared, not only becoming the core of global AI computing power, but also once topping the S&P 500 market capitalization. More importantly, what truly pushed the stock price to its extreme was not just institutions, but a continuous stream of retail investor funds.

But a very dangerous signal has appeared.

Retail investors have started selling.

Retail investors have started selling.

According to the latest data from StockWe.com, a US stock market data platform, individual investors were net sellers of Nvidia shares for the first time since July of last year on Wednesday. This is not a typical reduction in holdings, but a "behavioral turning point"—meaning that even the most steadfast buyers in the market are beginning to waver. The significance of this event is far greater than it appears on the surface. I. The core of the AI ​​market rally is actually "faith trading." Many people believe this round of gains is driven by fundamentals. However, if you break down the structure, you'll find: Institutions: Early positioning, later more about "following the trend." Retail investors: Continuous buying, the core source of marginal buying. In other words, the real fuel for the AI ​​bull market is the continuous buying by retail investors. And NVIDIA is the "faith core" of this system. Once this core begins to loosen, the market logic will change—from "buy on dips" to "take profits on rallies." II. Why Now? Three Key Variables Are Reversing This shift in retail investor sentiment is not a random event, but the result of three combined forces: 1) Geopolitical Conflicts Disrupt Risk Appetite Escalating tensions in the Middle East have abruptly shifted the market from the "AI narrative" back to "macroeconomic risks." When risks rise, the first assets to be abandoned are often those with the largest gains and highest valuations. And AI leaders are a prime example of "highly crowded trades."

2) AI Trading Enters the "Late Stage"

Any round of thematic market trends goes through three stages:

  • Storytelling (Expectations)

  • Looking at Growth (Realization)

  • Valuation Competition (Game Theory)

Current AI has clearly entered the third stage.

This means that:

The rise is no longer based on logic, but on the relay of funds.

Once funds start to hesitate, volatility will amplify dramatically.

3) Retail Investor Behavior Begins a "Reflexive Reversal"

Over the past year, retail investor behavior has been: buying → pushing up prices → attracting more buyers → further price increases.

But now, the chain is reversing: selling → depressing prices → triggering panic → more selling.

This is a typical **trend reflexive inflection point.

III. What does this mean? Not a crash, but the risk structure has changed

It must be made clear here:

This does not mean the end of the AI ​​rally, nor does it mean that NVIDIA will immediately plummet.

Commentary

The market never ends at its hottest point, but cracks will inevitably appear when "the most steadfast begin to waver."

And this time, the cracks have appeared among retail investors.

The AI ​​bull market is not over, but it is no longer safe.

data.title: Apple's 50 Years: Geniuses Retire, Machines Live On
data.description:
Author: Sleepy.md

In April 1976, three men signed a partnership agreement for Apple Inc. in a garage in California. Twelve days later, one of the men withdrew from the partnership. If he hadn't withdrawn and had weathered half a century, his 10% stake would be worth $400 billion today. That's enough for him to buy half of the Middle Eastern oil empire, or to easily surpass Elon Musk on the Forbes rich list.

That man's name was Ronald Wayne.

When people discuss Apple's 50-year history, they habitually glorify the perseverance of Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak, then casually mock Wayne's cowardice and shortsightedness in selling his shares for a mere $800. However, Wayne, then 41, was the only one of the three with a stable job, assets, and even a family. Jobs, on the other hand, had mortgaged everything to borrow money for parts. Looking at this long-haired, vacant-eyed young man, Wayne felt only unease. Because if the company went bankrupt, according to the partnership laws at the time, the creditors would spare the two penniless young men and then legally take every car, every house, and every penny of savings under Wayne's name. Wayne's departure was a rational calculation by an ordinary person facing "extreme uncertainty." He fled back to his safe life. Wayne left Apple out of fear of risk, but the irony of history is that Apple, in the following 50 years, became another Wayne. This company outwardly proclaims "Think Different," but at its core, it is extremely risk-averse. Wayne left Apple because of his aversion to risk; from then on, genius was responsible for creating myths, while the system was responsible for strangling uncertainty. Apple's 50 years are not just a story of "genius changing the world," but also a victory of the system over the individual, of calculation replacing inspiration. Early Apple relied on Jobs's personal heroism to combat risk, so how did this behemoth, once fully matured, use hundreds of billions of dollars to buy absolute security in the capital markets? "Hedge funds" disguised as tech companies. Jobs had an extreme aversion to dividends and stock buybacks. In his view, every penny Apple earned should be reinvested in research and development. Even in 2010, when Apple's cash reserves were already enormous, Jobs remained steadfast in his stance despite pressure from Wall Street. However, after Jobs' death, the new CEO, Tim Cook, succumbed to shareholder pressure and announced Apple's first-ever dividend and a multi-billion dollar stock buyback program on March 19, 2012. From that day on, in the eyes of Wall Street, Apple gradually transformed from a world-changing technology company into a "hedge fund" masquerading as a technology company. According to statistics from Creative Planning and various financial institutions, from 2013 to the end of 2024, Apple's total stock buyback amount reached $700.6 billion.

Among the constituent stocks of the S&P 500 index, this figure exceeds the total market capitalization of 488 companies. In other words, the money Apple uses to buy its own stock is enough to directly buy any publicly traded company outside the 13th largest in the world by market capitalization, such as Eli Lilly, Visa, or Netflix.

When we zoom out to the current AI frenzy, while Amazon, Google, and Meta are burning through cash on AI big data models and computing power, with total investments approaching $700 billion, trying to gamble on an uncertain future at a table where the cards are unclear, Apple is using an equal amount of money to buy its own stock. Technological innovation is risky; you might invest a hundred billion and not even hear a sound. But reducing outstanding shares and increasing earnings per share are 100% certain on the financial statements. Over the past decade, although Apple's net profit growth has slowed, its EPS has been artificially inflated by nearly 280% through aggressive share buybacks. Over the past few years, Warren Buffett has heavily invested in Apple, at one point even making it the absolute majority holding in Berkshire Hathaway's portfolio, exceeding 20%. What he wasn't buying was the growth potential of tech stocks; he was buying the absolute certainty that this sophisticated machine provided during a period of technological stagnation. In the mature stage of an industry cycle, financial engineering is far more lucrative and stable than technological research and development. It no longer needs a groundbreaking product to shock the world; it simply needs to act like a tireless pump, drawing profits and precisely channeling them into Wall Street's reservoir. On the financial statements, Apple bought absolute certainty for $700 billion. But how are the profits supporting this massive numbers game squeezed out from assembly lines in the physical world? In March, Tim Cook once again appeared in China with a beaming smile. Sipping Chinese afternoon tea, he smiled at the camera and said, "The Chinese supply chain is crucial to Apple; we couldn't have achieved what we have today without Chinese suppliers." But behind this warm and friendly public relations rhetoric, Apple is quietly undertaking an epic supply chain migration. By 2025, Apple will have assembled 55 million iPhones in India, a 53% increase from the previous year. This means that now, one out of every four iPhones produced globally comes from India. The Tata Group has just built a massive new factory in Hosur, Tamil Nadu, in southern India, with plans to double its workforce to 40,000. Meanwhile, Foxconn's factories in India exported $4.4 billion worth of iPhones to the United States in the first five months of 2025 alone, and the latest iPhone 17 series has achieved a breakthrough by assembling all models in India. The reasons behind the supply chain shift are not as simple as "finding cheaper labor." It's an operation performed by Apple's system to eliminate geopolitical uncertainty and the risk of single nodes. Apple designs its global supply chain like a motherboard; wherever there's risk, it removes the capacitor and plugs it into a safer place. In this process, whether it's the workers on the Foxconn assembly lines in China who once created the "Zhengzhou speed," or the young laborers in the Hosso factory in India who have just put on anti-static clothing, in Apple's system, there is essentially no difference. They are all just gears on this massive machine that are replaced seasonally. Apple cares about the stability and cost of the gears operating. It tightly controls product design at its spaceship headquarters in California, while perfectly outsourcing the dirty work of production and management conflicts to Foxconn and Tata. In this seemingly impenetrable supply chain system, all suppliers and workers are merely replaceable consumables. Having achieved this breathtaking control in the physical world, how will this behemoth repeat its tactics in the face of the most ferocious wave of AI in the digital world? The tollbooth leading to the gold mine. In 2024, the generative AI wave swept in, and ChatGPT made the entire Silicon Valley exclaim that the "iPhone moment" had arrived again. Analysts were mocking Apple: Siri was like an idiot, Apple was lagging behind in the AI ​​era, and Apple was doomed. But by 2026, when AI large-scale model companies were burning through cash for computing power and racking their brains for commercial monetization, data from AppMagic surprised everyone. In 2025, generative AI applications paid nearly $900 million in commissions to Apple just to be listed on the App Store—the so-called "Apple tax." Of this, nearly 75% was paid by ChatGPT alone. Musk's Grok ranked second, contributing 5%. This is what makes Apple so terrifying. While it didn't create the shovel for panning for gold, it directly controlled the only road to the gold mine and then built a tollbooth. Whether you're Claude or OpenAI, if you want to reach billions of high-net-worth iOS users worldwide, you have to obediently listen to Apple and hand over 30% (or 15%) of your revenue to Cook. Amidst the frenzied AI bubble, Apple, with an almost rogue-like ecosystem monopoly, forcibly transformed all AI innovations attempting to disrupt it into steadily growing service revenue on its financial statements. In the fourth quarter of fiscal year 2025, Apple's service revenue reached a record high of $28.8 billion, a 15% year-over-year increase. Of this, AI applications, seen by outsiders as Apple's disruptors, contributed the most lucrative portion of the profits. Of course, this kind of greed also attracted the hammer of antitrust scrutiny. On March 15, 2026, facing enormous regulatory pressure, Apple made rare concessions in the Chinese market, reducing the standard commission for the App Store from 30% to 25%, and the commission for small and micro developers from 15% to 12%. But this did not fundamentally damage its core business. From the physical world's supply chain to the digital world's App Store, Apple has mastered systematic control to an art form. When this machine reaches such pinnacle of precision, does the person sitting in the cockpit still need to be a genius? The Ultimate Victory of Cook and His Men On Apple's 50th anniversary, the biggest gossip in Silicon Valley isn't any revolutionary new product, but rather Cook's successor. All the clues point to one name: John Tenus. This 50-year-old Senior Vice President of Hardware Engineering at Apple is practically another Tim Cook. He graduated from the University of Pennsylvania in 1997 with a degree in mechanical engineering and joined Apple in 2001, where he stayed for 24 years. His resume is clean; there's none of the madness of Steve Jobs seeking a spiritual mentor in India, nor any of those unconventional anecdotes. A New York Times in-depth report recounted that when Tenus was promoted, the company offered him a private office with a door, but he refused. He chose to continue working in the open-plan office, which resembled a dormitory, alongside his engineering team. He was pragmatic, low-key, and extremely focused on teamwork. Even in key decisions like pushing for LiDAR on iPadOS and the iPhone Pro, he demonstrated a businessman's calculation of "finding an absolute balance between product definition and commercial interests." If Tenus successfully takes over, this will be Apple's final physical break from "individual heroism." The market is always fascinated by dreamers like Jobs; they descend like gods, cleaving through the chaos with dazzling light, showing you what the future looks like. But what truly supports the seamless operation of a four trillion dollar empire is Tim Cook, who meticulously manages every penny and every screw. When Tim Cook took over Apple, the company's market capitalization was $349 billion. Fifteen years later, amidst criticism for "lack of innovation," he propelled Apple's market capitalization to a peak of nearly $4 trillion, more than tenfold. He didn't rely on flashes of inspiration, but rather on meticulously squeezing the supply chain, mastering the use of financial buyback tools, and almost domineeringly profiting from the App Store ecosystem. The rise of Tenus signifies that Apple has completely abandoned its search for the next dreamer. The company has fully embraced Cook's philosophy: in the mature stage of the tech industry, mediocre operational geniuses are more crucial than brilliant product geniuses. We miss Jobs because we miss the era when technology could still get our hearts racing; we can't live without Cook because we've become accustomed to technology being as stable, mundane, but indispensable as tap water. Apple's 50 years began with Wayne, an ordinary man afraid of taking risks, and ended with an extremely sophisticated, massive, and uncertainty-averse super-system. It eliminated capital risk with a $700 billion buyback, eliminated manufacturing risk with a global supply chain relocation, eliminated the risk of technological change with App Store toll fees, and finally, eliminated the risk of "human" risk by having Tenus succeed Cook. At fifty years old, Apple has finally become the most ruthless, sophisticated, and profitable elder brother in the screen it smashed with a hammer in 1984. Geniuses retire, machines live on forever.

data.title: Nium Launches Platform Connecting Stablecoins to Visa and Mastercard for Everyday Payments
data.description:
Nium Lets Businesses Turn Stablecoins Into Spendable Cards Across Visa and Mastercard

Businesses can now issue payment cards funded by stablecoins and use digital dollar balances directly at merchants through existing card networks, thanks to Nium’s new platform.

The system converts stablecoin balances into fiat at the point of sale while managing settlement, compliance, and card network integration through a single interface.

Excited to partner with @NiumGlobal to scale stablecoin-linked cards https://t.co/hHB0JpFe0M

— Cuy Sheffield (@cuysheffield) March 30, 2026

How Does Nium Simplify Stablecoin Payments

By consolidating conversion, settlement, and compliance into one integration layer, Nium says it can reduce the time to launch stablecoin card programs from months to just days.

Companies no longer need separate systems or partnerships to handle each step, allowing faster deployment and smoother operations.

Transactions appear as standard card payments for consumers, while stablecoins are processed behind the scenes, bridging digital assets with traditional commerce rails.

Stablecoins Are Gaining Legislative Attention

Stablecoins currently hold a market value exceeding $315 billion, with Tether’s USDT representing roughly $184 billion, or 58% of the market, according to DefiLlama.

The Stablecoin Weekly: Another week, another quiet reminder that stablecoins aren't slowing down 👇

✜ $315B market cap which is up $2.6B WoW.

✜ USDT commands a $184B mrkt cap & a 58% dominance across all chains.

✜ Ethereum at $163B mrkt cap with Tron second at $86B.… pic.twitter.com/2NjxHTlJyy

— 0Fiat (@0FiatDotCom) March 16, 2026

Bain & Company noted that “stablecoins are having a headline moment as US legislators turn their attention to clarifying the rules of the game.”

The CLARITY Act, which aims to formalise stablecoin regulations, remains stalled in Congress as banks and the crypto industry debate reward mechanisms and oversight.

Are Card Networks Expanding Stablecoin Support

Despite regulatory uncertainty in the US, activity around stablecoin payments is growing.

Visa has expanded support to four tokens across four blockchains, enabling conversion into more than 25 fiat currencies.

Supported assets include USDC, Euro Coin, PayPal USD, and Global Dollar, across Ethereum, Solana, Avalanche, and Stellar.

Meanwhile, Mastercard agreed earlier this month to acquire stablecoin infrastructure firm BVNK in a deal worth up to $1.8 billion, aiming to link fiat systems with blockchain transactions.

VISA Launched In 1958. MASTERCARD Followed In 1966.
Together, They Process ~$24.8T Annually.

STABLECOINS Are Only About 6 Years Old And Already Surpassed That Figure. pic.twitter.com/UyahIchBHn

— Crypto Patel (@CryptoPatel) March 28, 2026

How PayPal Is Innovating With Dollar-Pegged Tokens

Beyond cards, PayPal launched its PYUSD stablecoin in August 2023 and recently introduced PYUSDx, a platform for developers to issue dollar-backed tokens for transactions within apps and digital ecosystems.

Cross-border payments don’t have to be painful.

PYUSD is rolling out to the PayPal wallet across 70 markets — enabling faster settlements, lower costs, and built for everyday global commerce.

Learn more: https://t.co/E90lc4vUsNpic.twitter.com/8yQhJ19Thp

— PayPal (@PayPal) March 17, 2026

These moves indicate a wider trend of stablecoins moving from digital wallets into mainstream payment channels.

Will Stablecoins Reshape Global Payments

Nium’s platform connects digital currency with trusted financial networks without requiring businesses to change the end-user experience.

By streamlining compliance, conversion, and settlement, it enables faster adoption of stablecoins in everyday commerce.

This approach could accelerate competition with traditional banking infrastructure and expand hybrid systems where fiat and blockchain coexist seamlessly across global payments.

data.title: Bitcoin tax loopholes are about to close, but stablecoins are poised to be the biggest winners.
data.description: Recently, the US Congress passed a new draft bill called the **Digital Asset PARITY Act**. The name sounds fair, advocating for equal treatment of assets, but upon closer inspection, it's far from equal; it's clearly handing a knife to Bitcoin investors while giving sugar to stablecoins. Let's first discuss what this bill actually aims to do. Simply put, it aims to **transfer the wash trading rules from the stock market to cryptocurrencies**. What are **wash trading rules**? It means that if you sell a stock at a loss and want to record a loss on your books to offset taxes, but then buy it back within 30 days before or after the sale, then unfortunately, this loss cannot be offset for taxes in that year and must be postponed. This set of rules has been in use in the stock market for decades, but cryptocurrencies have always been a legal loophole. Why? Because the IRS defines Bitcoin as property, while the legal provisions of the liquidation rules only mention stocks or securities, not digital assets. This creates a legal vacuum that investors can openly exploit. How? An example will make it clear. Suppose you bought 1 Bitcoin for $100,000 at the beginning of the year, and it dropped to $60,000 in the middle of the year. You sold it, losing $40,000. Then, five minutes later, you bought it back, still holding 1 Bitcoin, with no change in risk exposure. But when you file your taxes at the end of the year, this $40,000 loss can be used to offset other investment income or ordinary income. What is this called? This is called **Tax-Loss Harvesting**, a routine operation used annually by many US crypto investors. However, in the stock market, this operation is illegal, and losses are prohibited from being deducted in the current year. Now, Congress is closing this loophole. The bill explicitly extends the scope of the wash trading rule to actively traded digital assets, including Bitcoin, Ethereum, and their derivatives. From now on, if you want to recoup losses by selling at a loss and buying back, sorry, that path is no longer viable. This is one shoe in the bill. The other shoe falls on stablecoins. The bill specifically opens a loophole for regulated payment stablecoins: As long as the stablecoins you buy and sell meet certain conditions and the transaction price is between $0.99 and $1.01, you don't need to confirm profits or losses, nor do you need to calculate capital gains tax. Simply put, you can use stablecoins like cash without having to calculate taxes on every payment. This loophole is carefully designed. To enjoy this treatment, a stablecoin must meet several conditions: it must meet the definition of a payment stablecoin under the GENIUS framework, be issued by an approved institution, be pegged solely to the US dollar, have fluctuated around $1 for at least 95% of the trading days in the past year, and the price at which you buy it must also be around $1. Those meeting these conditions are basically regulated stablecoins like USDC and PYUSD. Whether USDT can be included depends on whether it can pass regulatory hurdles. So you see, the logic of this bill is very clear: **use taxation to divide crypto assets into two categories**. **One category is speculative trading, such as Bitcoin, which will be subject to laundering rules and will no longer be able to avoid taxes through losses.** The other category is payment instruments, regulated stablecoins, offering tax benefits and encouraging their use.** This reflects the regulators' consistent approach: **crack down on speculation and support payments**. The U.S. Treasury Department's 2025 Digital Asset Report explicitly recommended extending laundering rules to digital assets, but emphasized that it should not apply to payment stablecoins. Congress has now put this recommendation on paper. The stablecoin market is currently quite large, at approximately $316 billion, with trading volume exceeding $34 trillion last year. Interestingly, according to analysis by Wharton Business School and the World Economic Forum, roughly 99% of stablecoin activity is still related to digital asset trading, with very little actual use in payment scenarios. Congress's decision to give stablecoins the tax green light aims to reverse this trend and allow stablecoins to truly integrate into everyday payments. So, the question arises: how much impact will this bill have on Bitcoin investors? In the short term, those who like to engage in short-term trading and reap year-end losses will be the most affected. Previously, they could sell losing positions at the end of the year to offset tax losses, then buy them back the following year. This operation will no longer be possible; losses cannot be deducted in the current year and must be carried forward. Long-term holders will be less affected. If you buy Bitcoin and don't hold it, the wash trading rules won't apply to you. You'll only run into this red line if you frequently buy and sell, or if you're trying to do tax planning at the end of the year. However, this doesn't mean long-term holders are completely unaffected. If market liquidity decreases due to fewer short-term transactions, all participants will feel the chill. Moreover, strategies that previously relied on profit-taking to hedge against short-term trading losses will be less effective. Stablecoins, on the other hand, are a real boon. Previously, when you used stablecoins to buy things or transfer funds, you theoretically had to calculate profit or loss for every transaction. Although most people didn't actually do it, strictly speaking from a tax perspective, even a difference of a few cents between the cost of buying a stablecoin and the selling price constitutes a capital gain or loss. The bill now explicitly states that as long as the transaction price is around $1, profit or loss will not be confirmed; it will be calculated directly at $1. This removes a major obstacle for daily use. However, it's important to note that this stablecoin tax incentive will only take effect for tax years starting January 1, 2026, while the changes to the laundering rules will take effect immediately after the bill's passage. This means that actions to close loopholes may be implemented faster than those to provide convenience. The explanatory document for the draft also acknowledges that the stablecoin provisions are still under technical review; for example, whether to set a $200 threshold for a single transaction or an annual total limit has not yet been finalized. From an investor's perspective, this bill sends a clear signal: regulators are using tax tools to shape the industry structure. Those seeking speculation will face increasingly higher tax costs; those aiming for payment services will receive more and more policy benefits for compliant stablecoins. This isn't the first time the US has manipulated taxation. In 2025, the IRS introduced Form 1099-DA, requiring brokers to report digital asset transactions starting January 1, 2025, and to provide copies to taxpayers starting February 17, 2026. While taxpayers still need to calculate most of the transaction costs for 2025, the era of standardized reporting has arrived. This new bill further refines the tax treatment of transactions based on the reporting system. From a broader perspective, the US has consistently followed a path in cryptocurrency regulation: **distinguishing between assets and currencies**. **Bitcoin is considered a commodity and regulated by the CFTC; stablecoins are considered payment instruments and regulated by the OCC.** Now, tax policy is also beginning to follow this dividing line. Whether the bill will ultimately pass depends on the interplay between the two parties. Currently, there are representatives from both parties pushing for it, but specific clauses remain controversial. Banks and crypto companies are still arguing over the economic interests of stablecoins, and the implementation of the regulatory framework will take time. However, the direction is relatively clear: the redemption rules are less controversial and are likely to pass; the details regarding stablecoins still need polishing, but the direction is set. For ordinary investors, there are several things to do now: First, monitor the progress of the bill, especially when the redemption rules will take effect; second, reassess your trading strategies to see if they are still effective for year-end loss mitigation; third, keep a record of the cost basis of each transaction, as tax compliance requirements will become increasingly stringent. The core logic of this bill is simple: to use tax incentives to guide funds towards areas that regulators want to support. As for Bitcoin, it will be treated the same as stocks from now on; don't expect to avoid taxes by selling at a loss and buying back in. The name is "equality," but it's actually a transfer. It's transferring Bitcoin's tax advantages to stablecoins. Suppress one, support another. This is the true intention of the Digital Asset Parity Act.

data.title: Strategy Breaks 13-Week Bitcoin Buying Streak, Raising Questions Over Saylor’s Conviction
data.description:
Saylor’s Bitcoin engine suddenly stalls

Strategy, long seen as Bitcoin’s most relentless corporate buyer, has halted its BTC purchases for the first time in 13 weeks — a surprising pause that comes amid a sharp stock decline and growing signs that the broader industry may be pivoting away from pure Bitcoin accumulation.

In its latest filing with the SEC, Strategy revealed that it made no Bitcoin purchases and no stock sales between March 23 and March 29 — breaking a consistent weekly buying streak that had become a hallmark of Michael Saylor’s aggressive treasury strategy.

However, the company still holds 762,099 BTC, valued at over $51 billion, but the lack of new buying marks a notable shift in behavior. Historically, Strategy has funded its Bitcoin accumulation through continuous sales of its Class A shares, making last week’s inactivity particularly striking.

Strategy acquired 1,031 BTC for $76.6 million at $74,326 per coin.

Total holdings: 762,099 BTC. Average cost basis: $75,694. 104 purchases.

Strategy is approaching 90,000 Bitcoin purchased this quarter alone. pic.twitter.com/iP0nSYFHLP

— TFTC (@TFTC21) March 23, 2026

The pause coincides with mounting pressure on Strategy’s stock, which has plunged more than 60% over the past six months to around $126.78. At the same time, Bitcoin itself has fallen roughly 18% over the past year, adding further strain to the firm’s high-conviction bet.

Notably, Saylor — known for his vocal and frequent Bitcoin commentary — has remained silent on the move. That silence is fueling speculation: is this merely a tactical pause, or the first crack in one of crypto’s most unwavering bullish narratives?

Industry pivot intensifies as pressure builds

Strategy’s unexpected halt comes as other crypto-focused companies begin to rethink their approach to Bitcoin exposure. Across the sector, firms are increasingly shifting toward alternative strategies, including AI infrastructure and high-performance computing, in search of more stable or diversified revenue streams.

One of the clearest examples is MARA Holdings, which sold 15,133 BTC in March for approximately $1.1 billion, using the proceeds to reduce debt. The move signals a decisive shift away from pure accumulation toward balance sheet management and operational resilience.

BTC第二大矿企，甩卖BTC套现10+亿美金。

MARA这个月卖了15133枚比特币，套现11亿美金。

Mara“背叛”btc，晚晚我觉得也是好事。

1.挖矿现在是个赔本买卖。

矿企现在基本都是「即挖即卖」，心照不宣而已。
挖了就亏钱，不卖更亏死。

2.卖了币干嘛

Mara也是搞AI数据中心。…

— 林晚晚的猫 (@linwanwan823) March 26, 2026

At the same time, not all players are retreating. Canaan continues to expand its crypto holdings and mining operations, reporting reserves of 1,793 BTC and 3,952 ETH while growing its presence in Texas. This divergence highlights a market at a crossroads, where conviction and caution are increasingly colliding.

Against this backdrop, Strategy’s $51 billion Bitcoin reserve still stands as the largest corporate treasury in the world. However, the company’s first buying pause in months arrives at a critical moment — one where rising costs, falling prices, and shifting industry dynamics are forcing even the most committed players to reassess.

BTC第二大矿企，甩卖BTC套现10+亿美金。

MARA这个月卖了15133枚比特币，套现11亿美金。

Mara“背叛”btc，晚晚我觉得也是好事。

1.挖矿现在是个赔本买卖。

矿企现在基本都是「即挖即卖」，心照不宣而已。
挖了就亏钱，不卖更亏死。

2.卖了币干嘛

Mara也是搞AI数据中心。…

— 林晚晚的猫 (@linwanwan823) March 26, 2026

Whether this signals a temporary breather or the beginning of a deeper strategic pivot remains to be seen. But for a company that built its identity on unwavering Bitcoin accumulation, even a single week of inaction is enough to send ripples across the market.

data.title: Is the end of SaaS coming? The bullish logic behind software stocks.
data.description:
Author: Anthony J. Pompliano, Founder and CEO of Professional Capital Management; Translated by: Shaw & Jinse Finance

The term "SaaS doomsday theory" has quickly become popular on Wall Street. Since last September, the S&P North American Technology Software Index has lost 32% of its market capitalization. Such a huge drop in just a few months is extremely rare.

Salesforce's stock price has fallen by more than 26%, Adobe's market capitalization has shrunk by 20%, and the stock prices of dozens of mid-cap SaaS companies have been halved. If you have been following enterprise software stocks recently, you will have noticed that the entire sector is undergoing a complete revaluation. For investors holding these stocks, the performance of their holdings is undoubtedly worrying.

But what if everyone panics and prepares to cut their losses and exit at the bottom of the pullback? What if the worst is over? I don't have a crystal ball to predict the future, nor do I intend to accurately predict the market bottom, but contrarian investors would argue that the market is overreacting, the mainstream bearish logic contradicts reality, and investors entering the market now may be facing the best opportunity to position themselves once in a decade. Before discussing future trends, let's clarify why the market has reached this point. This round of decline was caused by two distinct shocks. The first was the impact of the macroeconomy. In 2022, the Federal Reserve raised interest rates at the fastest pace in history, severely impacting duration assets. Few assets have a duration exceeding that of high-growth software companies—their cash flow valuations often project a decade into the future. Taking the Bethesda Cloud Index as an example, the median revenue valuation of listed SaaS companies plummeted from a historical peak of 18.4 times forward revenue in September 2021 to approximately 6 times in early 2026. This adjustment alone was extremely drastic. This valuation metric alone has plunged by 65%. The second wave of impact comes from structural changes in the industry. Frankly, this is what has caused even greater panic among investors. The core reason can be summarized as follows: the rise of AI intelligent agents has led to what analysts call seat compression—that is, a single AI agent can perform the work of multiple employees, thus reducing the number of software licenses required by enterprises. After Atlassian first announced a decline in the number of corporate seats, its stock price plummeted by 35%. Workday announced 8.5% layoffs, directly citing AI as one of the reasons. If these examples aren't alarming enough, a CIO survey in January 2026 shows that enterprise IT budget growth is projected to be only 3.4%. Most analysts believe this significant slowdown in budget growth is due to funds being diverted to AI infrastructure construction by hyperscale cloud vendors, with planned spending exceeding $660 billion. This figure is undoubtedly staggering, and once the market sees such massive funding coupled with panic selling, it quickly triggers a sell-off. This was precisely the market trend at the beginning of this year. A typical manifestation was that enterprise price-to-sales ratios compressed from 9 times to about 6 times within weeks, a level that is the lowest since the mid-2010s. However, it's worth noting that the decline in software stocks is not uniform. A clear divergence has emerged between AI infrastructure software and traditional application software. Palantir's stock price surged 135% in 2025 due to a 121% year-over-year increase in US commercial revenue, while also providing a revenue guidance of $7.2 billion for fiscal year 2026, significantly exceeding analysts' expectations. High-quality companies with genuine growth can almost defy market conditions and navigate any cycle. Similar examples include: Microsoft Azure's quarterly revenue exceeding $50 billion, with a year-over-year growth rate of 39%; Oracle Cloud Infrastructure's quarterly growth of 84%, and the announcement of a $553 billion backlog of orders. A company with a backlog of nearly a trillion dollars is hard not to consider more valuable in the future. Now let's compare this to the other end of the market. Salesforce is a lamentable example, with its market value evaporating by more than a quarter; Adobe's annual revenue growth rate is still 12%, but its forward P/E ratio has been compressed to about 10—such a low valuation usually means the company has entered a permanent decline. The situation with CrowdStrike is even more perplexing: as a recognized structural winner in the cybersecurity field, its market position is becoming increasingly important with the proliferation of AI, yet its valuation is 20% lower than the five-year average price-to-sales ratio. From these cases, I draw the core conclusion that the market is treating all non-infrastructure software as a liability. Contrarian investors believe this is precisely the market's mistake. So where exactly is the problem? The mainstream bearish view holds that AI will disrupt the SaaS industry. Pessimists claim that enterprises (especially large corporations) will stop paying for software subscriptions because AI agents will handle a large amount of work. Is this possible? Of course. But based on the current facts, it's by no means an obvious conclusion. Contrarian investors might point out a fatal flaw in this logic: Industry leaders aren't sitting idly by; they're building their own AI layers, leveraging their two decades of accumulated exclusive enterprise data, customer relationships, and distribution channels to create advantages that startups can't replicate in a short time. In short, traditional giants are only disrupted if they don't proactively innovate. Take Salesforce as an example: its Agentforce AI platform achieved $800 million in annual recurring revenue in fiscal year 2026, a surge of 169% year-over-year, and is currently transitioning to a pay-as-you-go, results-based pricing model. If this model scales successfully, startups will find it difficult to compete with Salesforce's channel advantages. Another noteworthy example is ServiceNow's generative AI suite, Now Assist, whose annual contract value has already exceeded $600 million and is expected to reach $1 billion by the end of the year. A $1 billion contract is a significant sum. Not to mention software industry giant Microsoft. It launched a new Microsoft 365 Enterprise plan, priced at $99 per user per month, 65% higher than its previous highest plan, aiming to directly monetize AI value through existing users. Therefore, despite the clamor of bearish voices, these cases do not threaten the SaaS business model; rather, they signify that the SaaS model is evolving into a more powerful form, directly benefiting from the AI ​​technology wave. Another key point is that many companies are shifting from a per-user licensing model to a usage-based billing model—no longer charging per person, but rather based on completed tasks or delivered results. This business model iteration, coupled with the expected 15% growth in enterprise software spending this year, makes the pessimistic arguments about software stocks increasingly untenable. The companies best positioned to absorb this incremental spending are precisely the industry leaders who already possess strong enterprise customer relationships, compliance systems, and workflow integration capabilities. Bain & Company's research also confirms that the vast majority of customers prefer to purchase AI-enabled solutions from existing suppliers. This makes sense: as long as the technology and cost are competitive, companies will prioritize familiar partners and trusted brands. So, what conditions need to be met to discredit the bears and restore confidence in software stocks? This situation is slightly more complex, and there is no easy solution. The market needs several key factors to change. First, AI monetization must break through a credibility threshold: if Agentforce's annual recurring revenue exceeds $1 billion, market confidence in Salesforce will recover; ServiceNow's Now Assist needs to achieve $1 billion in annual contract value; and Microsoft needs to demonstrate that Copilot can consistently and quantifiably increase average revenue per user. These data are likely to be released as early as the second half of this year, at which point the market narrative will shift from "AI disrupting SaaS" to "SaaS monetizing through AI." Secondly, IT budget data from multiple companies needs to be verified. Even with a reduction in available seats, software spending still saw net growth. This is the most concrete evidence to refute the bearish logic. Finally, a stable macroeconomic environment is crucial. If interest rate hikes continue, the sector will continue to be under pressure; however, if interest rates remain unchanged or even begin to cut, the pressure of valuation compression will gradually dissipate, and a genuine upward revaluation will begin. If the bears are wrong, how much can investors gain in the future? The good news is that the valuations of high-quality software stocks are already at their most attractive levels in years. Microsoft's forward P/E ratio is approximately 24, with an expected annualized earnings growth of 14%. The average Wall Street target price is $600, representing about a 50% upside from the current share price. Cloudflare is similar, with an average analyst target price of $245, representing about a 40% upside from the current price. Snowflake's revenue growth is still at 29% year-over-year, its P/S ratio is 13, and consensus estimates indicate a 43% upside. These upside potentials are substantial and do not yet include the most optimistic analyst targets. For those seeking extreme asymmetric returns, contrarian investors would consider Adobe the best option. Its current forward P/E ratio is approximately 10, and its revenue and profit are still maintaining double-digit growth. As mentioned earlier, the market has priced it in as a company in structural recession, while it continues to grow. If Adobe's valuation returns to a normal P/E ratio of 25, the valuation repair alone would represent a gain of approximately 150%, not even factoring in earnings growth. In the US stock market, such cases abound. Multiple analysts predict that high-quality software stocks that successfully monetize AI could achieve a 40%-50% increase simply through valuation repair, not even considering the earnings growth brought about by technological innovation. It's important to remember that the market's reaction to the "SaaS doomsday" has been excessive, even believing that the entire industry will be disrupted to the point of insignificance. However, the data tells a completely different story: Perhaps SaaS companies are not being disrupted by external forces, but rather evolving internally into a more valuable form—higher revenue per customer, lower marginal delivery costs, and a potential market size that will double in the next four years. If this is indeed the case, the recent investor panic has already resulted in a "limited-time discount" on the entry price for these companies. The key question is, in hindsight, whether this panic was truly justified.

data.title: The Wall Street Journal exposes the inside story of Sora's shutdown: Is the OpenAI bubble about to burst?
data.description:
On March 24th, Sam Altman sent an email to internal employees announcing the shutdown of Sora. There was no grand farewell, no roadmap, no "we'll be back with a better product." Only one sentence: We need to use computing power for more important things.

Then the Wall Street Journal published an exclusive big news, exposing the inside story of Sora's shutdown~

Sora's direct cause of death was its excessively high costs. Sora burned through approximately one million dollars in computing power costs every day. Its peak user base was less than one million, and it continued to decline thereafter, with fewer than 500,000 active users before its shutdown.

The total revenue throughout the product's lifecycle was only two million one hundred thousand dollars.

While Disney had committed a $1 billion investment to the partnership, not only providing financial support but also licensing Sora to use IPs such as Mickey Mouse, Iron Man, and Darth Vader.

The direct cause of Sora's shutdown and the partnership blunder

Sora's direct cause of death was its excessively high costs. Sora burned through approximately one million dollars in computing power costs every day. Its peak user base was less than one million, and it continued to decline, with active users falling short of five hundred thousand before its shutdown.

The total revenue throughout the product's lifecycle was only two million one hundred thousand dollars.

Disney had committed a $1 billion investment to the partnership, providing not only financial support but also licensing Sora to use IPs such as Mickey Mouse, Iron Man, and Darth Vader ...>

No wonder Disney is so excited about Seedance's development...

OpenAI executives once likened this deal to "the end of the silent film era with sound films."

OpenAI executives once compared this deal to "sound films ending the silent film era."

However, Disney only learned of Sora's impending shutdown less than an hour before the announcement, a rather embarrassing situation... OpenAI has lost all its technological and strategic advantages. While Sora was shut down, ByteDance's Seedance was thriving, with independent evaluations showing Seedance leading across multiple performance metrics, including cue word adherence and multi-camera consistency. OpenAI not only lost to its competitors in video technology but was also strategically crushed by rivals. While the entire OpenAI team was still struggling with Sora, Claude Code was stealing software engineers and enterprise clients. OpenAI once possessed a special power: whatever it released defined the boundaries of possibility for the industry. The Bubble Structure of the Largest Private Equity Funding Round in History In the same month that Sora shut down, OpenAI completed a funding round. $110 billion in size, valued at $840 billion, the largest private equity funding round in history, bar none.

Moreover, the structure of this financing itself reflects a huge bubble:

  • Amazon committed $50 billion, but only $15 billion is immediate cash, with the remaining $35 billion subject to conditions—according to sources, the conditions may include OpenAI achieving specific AI capability milestones or completing an IPO by the end of 2026. Nvidia's $30 billion is primarily a GPU computing power commitment, not a cash equity investment. In other words, this money is essentially a "pre-purchase of GPUs." SoftBank's $30 billion was initially planned to be raised through bridge loans and financing from major financial institutions. To raise funds, SoftBank has begun selling existing holdings, including Nvidia. Therefore, as of the end of March, OpenAI had only received $25 billion in cash: $15 billion from Amazon + $10 billion from SoftBank's first installment. AI Bubble Could Collapse US Stocks Currently, SpaceX (which merged with XAI), OpenAI, and Anthropic, the three major AI giants, are all preparing for their IPOs this year, and their valuations all exceed one trillion US dollars! Before the internet was supposed to change the world, it first trapped Nasdaq investors for over a decade. Amazon's stock price plummeted from $107 to $6, a 94% drop; Microsoft's stock didn't recover its 1999 high until 2016. Wouldn't it be fair to say that this AI bubble will trap investors for another ten or eight years?

data.title: The real power struggle behind the CLARITY Act
data.description:
Author: Charlie Liu, Partner, Generative Ventures

Last week,A new draft of the CLARITY Act leaked, causing Circle to drop by about 20% in a single day, and Coinbase to drop by nearly 10%. ...>
